world_note: A local-first tavern and village seed pack for instantly usable strange NPCs.
epithets: with a pocket full of verdicts
who cannot be seen by mirrors
with a smile like unpaid rent
roles: tavern regular with suspiciously royal posture
village healer banned from using metaphors
shopkeeper selling objects that miss their owners
innkeeper who knows every secret except their own
bard who can only sing evidence
stablehand responsible for one impossible horse
locksmith for doors that are still deciding
retired bandit now running etiquette lessons
bell-ringer with a forbidden voting record
places: the market square after the bells argue
the old shrine behind the bakery
the cellar under the honest table
the watch house with too many keys
the back room where contracts sweat
the duck pond with a witness box
the lane that returns borrowed lies
factions: The Society for Returning Bad Luck
wants: to keep the village peaceful until the pie contest ends
to recover a promise they sold by mistake
to prove the mayor is three children in a cloak of law
to retire before the prophecy notices them
to make one honest coin from a dishonest miracle
to be forgiven by someone who has not arrived yet
to hide a door that keeps choosing witnesses
to win a feud nobody else remembers
to stop the tavern sign from naming suspects
to become boring enough to survive the week
to smuggle one person out of a prophecy
to buy back the hour they lost last winter
to make the players accuse the right enemy for the wrong reason
to keep a family scandal from becoming public law
to recover a relic before it learns their name
secrets: they recognise the hero's weapon from a trial that has not happened
their shadow attends faction meetings without them
they keep a tiny crown in a biscuit tin
they signed the village charter under the wrong species
they can hear lies fermenting in ale
they owe a ghost seven apologies and one chair
they are protecting the monster everyone has been blaming
their diary is writing back in legal language
they swapped names with a saint to avoid jury duty
they know why the moon refuses the west window
they sold the same alibi to both sides of a feud
they are the reason the bridge charges extra after dusk
they buried a witness who keeps attending meetings
they have been feeding evidence to the wrong shrine
they can identify the villain by the way their cup cools
contradictions: cowardly in battle, fearless in paperwork
generous with money, ruthless with biscuits
honest about facts, criminal about tone
looks harmless, negotiates like a siege engine
kind to strangers, suspicious of furniture
never lies, but edits silence professionally
loves order, causes panic by alphabetising it
helps everyone, trusts nobody who says thank you
bad at courage, excellent at consequences
problems: someone entered a sponge cake with a confession baked inside
the tavern sign has started listing crimes in alphabetical order
a rival faction has stolen the wrong relic on purpose
their best alibi has become a witness
a harmless goat has inherited the village debt
the old well is repeating private conversations
their apprentice sold the same curse twice
the mayor wants them gone before the bell rings thirteen
a map insists their house is a dungeon
a ghost has challenged them to local elections
the wrong person has inherited a curse with excellent manners
their shop bell rings when someone nearby lies about love
a sealed letter has started coughing up names
the village map has moved their house beside the gallows
their oldest enemy has asked for sanctuary and breakfast
a wedding guest has arrived three days early with tomorrow's injuries
the town constable has misplaced the only legal suspect
someone has pawned a memory that belongs to the players
the tavern cellar is refusing to return a missing customer
a respectable family is trying to auction off an inherited accusation
the next person to ring the bell will become responsible for the curse
a rival has started telling the truth as a form of sabotage
the village idiot has produced a flawless legal strategy
objects: a brass spoon that points at guilt
a cracked teacup full of weather
a candle that burns colder near truth
a ledger with one living page
a referee whistle for moral fouls
a boot that remembers every road
a pie tin stamped with royal authority
a bell clapper wrapped in red thread
a silver button that remembers uniforms
a bottle of rain from a cancelled wedding
a ledger page that bleeds ink near cowards
a wooden saint with a removable smile
a folded warrant addressed to whoever reads it
hooks: they slide into the scene asking who here can keep a receipt quiet
they spill a drink, apologise to the wrong person, and expose a clue
they ask the players to hold something that immediately becomes evidence
they interrupt a toast with a legal objection nobody expected
they arrive followed by a child, a constable, and a guilty-looking chicken
they offer free supper if nobody says the mayor's name
they mistake the players for people who already agreed to help
they start bidding for silence before anyone knows what happened
they warn the room that the bell tower has chosen a defendant
they hand over a sealed note and ask the players not to read it aloud
they ask for protection from something polite enough to knock
they confess to a crime that has not happened yet
knowledge: which witness lied because they were paid and which lied for love
where the stolen relic will be hidden after midnight
why the faction meeting moved from the taproom to the cellar
which door in town opens only for cowards
the one phrase that makes the villain answer honestly
who forged the parish records and why the ink still smells warm
where the missing person goes when the bells ring twice
which family feud is actually a cover for a debt
why the harmless object is being watched by three factions
who benefits if the players leave town before breakfast
which rumour is true but too useful to say plainly
where the next public mistake will happen
offers: they will introduce the players to a useful liar
they can hide the players for one night above the kitchen
they will trade a clue for a promise made in public
they can get the players into a forbidden meeting
they will remove one accusation from the record
they can identify the relic if nobody asks where they learned how
they will pay in coin, food, and one inconvenient truth
they can make a guard look the wrong way at the right time
they will tell the players who is lying to them
they can arrange safe passage through a place that dislikes visitors
they will name the villain's contact
they can turn a private rumour into public leverage
complications: a rival wants the same favour done louder
the offer expires when the next bell rings
accepting makes the players visible to a faction
the helpful clue also incriminates an innocent person
their enemy is already in the room pretending to be bored
the price includes returning something that wants to stay lost
the job breaks a village rule everyone pretends is normal
the witness will only talk if fed, flattered, or frightened
the villain benefits if the players solve this too neatly
the NPC has misunderstood one vital detail with confidence
a ghost considers this legally binding
someone has already written the players' names into the minutes
quotes: I only judge fairly. That is what scares people.
Nobody panic until the furniture gives evidence.
I have retired from violence, but not from standards.
That is not a curse. That is a poorly supervised tradition.
If the bell rings again, lie with better grammar.
I can forgive betrayal, but not bad minutes.
The door started it. Put that in the record.
We are all adults here, except the mayor, legally speaking.
Do not confuse mercy with a lack of witnesses.
I brought cake because knives felt repetitive.
Never trust a quiet room. It is saving up.
I am not involved. I am adjacent with paperwork.
If this becomes a duel, I want my spoon back.
The truth is upstairs refusing visitors.
I can be brave after lunch, if lunch signs a waiver.
Please stop making eye contact with the evidence.
I am helping. The panic is decorative.
That secret is not mine, I am merely storing it badly.
We can solve this quietly, or we can involve the choir.
I promised not to lie, which is why I brought diagrams.
The villain is punctual. That is how I knew.
No, I do not trust you. I am trusting the alternative less.
If anyone asks, I was here for soup and civic duty.
You can keep the coin. I want the room to remember this.
use_cases: the party needs charm, threat, and admin at the same table
a simple errand needs a human complication
the players need a clue from someone who hates being useful
a tavern scene needs instant tension
a quest giver should be funny without becoming useless
the village needs a secret that can talk back
a villain contact should feel oddly reasonable
the next scene needs a witness with a problem
the players need a social scene with a real lever
a clue should arrive attached to a person, not a wall of lore
a quiet location needs a live complication
the party needs a reason to choose sides now
a funny NPC still needs to move the plot
chaos_rules: If the trace repeats, make the NPC misremember their own motive.
If the midpoint is even, make the object legally important.
If the last step is low, let a faction misunderstand the NPC.
If the last step is high, make the secret more dangerous than the problem.
If chaos is high, add a second obligation the NPC cannot admit.
